title: Human relationships: personal relationships
summary: Personal relationships are one of the richest topics in the Human Relationships option. This lesson explains how relationships FORM through biological factors (attraction, pheromones, reward), cognitive factors (similarity) and sociocultural factors (proximity and familiarity); the role communication plays in keeping them going; and why relationships change or end (equity theory and breakdown models). Every claim is anchored to a named study you can actually USE in a Paper 2 essay, and the lesson finishes with a full 22-mark model answer mapped to the real IB markband.
learningObjectives: Explain the formation of personal relationships across the three levels of analysis: biological (attraction, pheromones, reward), cognitive (similarity) and sociocultural (proximity, familiarity).
Explain the role of communication in maintaining personal relationships, using named research.
Discuss why relationships change or end, using equity theory and a breakdown model.
Select, describe and USE supporting studies (aim, procedure, findings) as evidence rather than decoration.
Structure and evaluate a Paper 2 essay against the five ERQ criteria (out of 22).
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Form it, keep it, or lose it
simpleExplanation.summary: A personal relationship has three chapters. It starts (formation) for reasons that are partly biological, partly cognitive and partly sociocultural. It is kept alive (maintenance) mostly through how partners communicate. And it can change or end (dissolution) when the balance of fairness or the quality of communication breaks down. Your job in Paper 2 is to explain those chapters WITH research, not just describe the research.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a relationship like a campfire. Getting it lit depends on several conditions at once — a spark (biological attraction), dry tinder that catches easily because it is similar (cognitive similarity), and simply being close enough to the fire to feed it (sociocultural proximity). Keeping it burning depends on tending it — adding fuel and clearing the smoke, which is communication. And it goes out when one person stops adding wood while the other keeps giving everything (inequity), or when the smoke of criticism and contempt smothers the flame.
steps: Read the command term first: 'Explain' wants a well-supported account; 'Discuss' and 'Evaluate' want that PLUS developed critical thinking.
Pick the FACTORS the question asks about, then choose one strong study per factor that you can describe and link.
For every study, state aim, procedure and findings, then say the sentence that turns description into USE: 'This supports the claim that...'.
Add developed critical thinking — methodology, culture, alternative explanations, or contrasting two factors — and finish with a thesis-driven conclusion.

flashcards.back: A close, interdependent bond between two people, marked by mutual affection, trust and commitment. In IB terms it is studied through formation, maintenance (communication) and dissolution.
The idea that body odour linked to major histocompatibility complex (MHC) genes influences attraction, so we prefer partners whose immune genes differ from ours. Key study: Wedekind (1995).
Attraction is reinforced by the brain's reward system (dopamine, VTA activation). We are drawn to people whose presence is rewarding, so liking can be conditioned by association.
We are attracted to people we perceive as similar to us in attitudes, values and personality, because similarity is validating and reduces cognitive conflict. Key study: Markey & Markey (2007).
Physical and functional closeness increases interaction, which breeds familiarity; the mere-exposure effect then turns familiarity into liking. Key study: Festinger, Schachter & Back (1950).

AIM: test whether MHC affects odour preference. PROCEDURE: 49 women rated the odour of T-shirts worn for two nights by MHC-typed men. FINDINGS: women preferred odours of MHC-DISSIMILAR men — but the preference reversed for women on the contraceptive pill.

AIM: examine how physical proximity shapes friendship. PROCEDURE: mapped friendships in a student housing complex (Westgate). FINDINGS: next-door neighbours became friends far more often than distant residents; 'functional distance' (e.g. living near stairs/mailboxes) predicted popularity.

AIM: test whether people want partners similar to themselves. PROCEDURE: surveyed young adults on their ideal partner and, for couples, actual partner traits. FINDINGS: participants desired partners similar to themselves in warmth and dominance, and similar couples reported greater satisfaction — support for the similarity/cognitive factor.

Satisfaction depends on perceived FAIRNESS, not just personal profit. Both the under-benefitted (who feel resentful) and the over-benefitted (who feel guilty) are less satisfied than partners in an equitable relationship. Inequity drives change or breakdown.

Criticism, Contempt, Defensiveness and Stonewalling — communication patterns that predict dissolution. Contempt is the strongest single predictor. Illustrates communication as a maintenance/breakdown factor.

Breakdown as a process, not an event: Intrapsychic (private dissatisfaction) → Dyadic (confronting the partner) → Social (going public) → Grave-dressing (building a story of the end). Descriptive rather than causal.

flashcards.front: Exam term: USE vs describe research
flashcards.back: Describing = stating a study's findings. USING = adding a link sentence that shows HOW those findings support (or challenge) the argument. Criterion C rewards USE, not description.

quickCheck.prompt: Wedekind's (1995) T-shirt odour study is best used as evidence for which factor in relationship formation?
Festinger, Schachter & Back (1950) found that next-door neighbours became friends far more often than distant residents. This most directly supports:
options: The sociocultural factor of proximity and familiarity
quickCheck.answer: The sociocultural factor of proximity and familiarity

quickCheck.prompt: 'We are drawn to partners who share our attitudes and personality.' This claim reflects:
According to equity theory, who is LEAST satisfied?
options: The partner who feels the relationship is fair and balanced
The under-benefitted partner who puts in more than they get out
The partner with the higher income
The partner who talks the most
quickCheck.answer: The under-benefitted partner who puts in more than they get out

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes a paragraph that states Festinger's findings in full but never connects them back to the question. In IB terms this paragraph mainly costs marks in:
options: Criterion A (Focus), because the question is off-topic
Criterion C (Use of research), because research is DESCRIBED, not USED
Criterion E (Clarity), because the writing is disorganised
No criterion — describing a study is always enough
quickCheck.answer: Criterion C (Use of research), because research is DESCRIBED, not USED

faq.q: Do I have to cover all three levels of analysis (biological, cognitive, sociocultural) in a formation essay?
faq.a: No. The command is usually 'Discuss factors', and the markband rewards DEPTH over coverage. Two well-chosen factors, each with a study that is thoroughly explained and used, will out-score a thin tour of all three. Pick factors you can develop critically.

faq.q: Is similarity or complementarity the correct explanation for attraction?
faq.a: The stronger, better-supported claim in this option is SIMILARITY: we tend to be attracted to and stay satisfied with partners similar to us in attitudes and personality (Markey & Markey, 2007). Complementarity (attraction to opposites) is a popular belief but has weak empirical support, so it is best mentioned as a critical counterpoint rather than a main argument.

faq.q: Equity theory or social exchange theory — which do I use for why relationships end?
faq.a: They are related but distinct. Social exchange theory says we stay if rewards exceed costs (personal profit). Equity theory refines this: it is perceived FAIRNESS that matters, so even a profitable relationship can fail if one partner feels chronically over- or under-benefitted. For 'why relationships change or end', equity theory usually gives you richer critical points.

sections.content: Personal relationships are among the most reliable sources of happiness — and distress — in human life, which is exactly why the IB option studies them scientifically rather than romantically. In this lesson we build a complete arc: how relationships FORM (biological, cognitive and sociocultural factors), how communication KEEPS them going, and why they CHANGE or END. Throughout, the emphasis is not on collecting studies but on using them, because in Paper 2 the difference between a middle answer and a top answer is almost never how much you know — it is whether your research is put to work.
Formation 1 — biological factors: attraction, pheromones and reward

The biological level treats attraction as partly rooted in physiology and evolved dispositions rather than free choice. One strand is pheromonal: the major histocompatibility complex (MHC), a cluster of immune-system genes, appears to influence body odour, and there is evidence that we find the scent of MHC-dissimilar people more pleasant. The adaptive logic is that pairing with a genetically different partner would give offspring a broader immune repertoire. A second strand is reward: attraction is reinforced when another person's presence activates the brain's dopaminergic reward circuitry (for example the ventral tegmental area), so we come to like people whose company is pleasurable and to associate them with positive feeling — a conditioning account of liking.

items: **Key study (pheromones): Wedekind (1995).** AIM: test whether MHC affects odour preference. PROCEDURE: 49 women rated the odour of T-shirts worn for two nights by men who had been MHC-typed. FINDINGS: women rated the odours of MHC-DISSIMILAR men as more pleasant — a preference that reversed for women taking the contraceptive pill.
**LINK (use it):** this supports the claim that a biological, odour-mediated mechanism biases attraction toward genetically complementary partners, i.e. formation is not purely a conscious choice.

**Reward mechanism:** neuroimaging of early-stage romantic love shows activation of dopamine-rich reward regions, consistent with the idea that we are drawn to partners whose presence is intrinsically rewarding.

**Evaluation:** biological accounts can be reductionist and deterministic — they explain a bias, not the whole story, and struggle to explain relationships not oriented toward reproduction. The pill reversal in Wedekind's data also shows the mechanism is not fixed, which is itself a useful critical point.

sections.content: The cognitive level focuses on how we perceive and appraise potential partners. The best-supported cognitive factor is similarity: we are drawn to — and stay more satisfied with — people we perceive as similar to ourselves in attitudes, values and personality. Cognitively, similarity is rewarding because it validates our own views (someone who agrees with us confirms that we are 'right'), reduces the friction of disagreement, and makes another person feel predictable and easy to understand. This is often contrasted with the folk belief that 'opposites attract' (complementarity), which has consistently weaker support.

items: **Key study (similarity): Markey & Markey (2007).** AIM: test whether people want partners similar to themselves. PROCEDURE: surveyed young adults about their ideal partner and, among couples, their actual partners' traits. FINDINGS: participants described an ideal partner similar to themselves (especially in warmth and dominance), and couples who were more similar reported greater satisfaction.
**LINK (use it):** this supports similarity as a cognitive driver of both formation and satisfaction — we select and stay with partners who match our self-concept.

**Evaluation:** self-report and correlational designs cannot establish that similarity CAUSES attraction — attraction may create perceived similarity (we assume liked people agree with us). Findings may also reflect Western, individualistic samples where partner choice is comparatively free.

sections.content: Formation 3 — sociocultural factors: proximity and familiarity

The sociocultural level highlights the surprisingly ordinary conditions that make relationships possible. Chief among them is proximity: we form relationships with the people we are physically and functionally near, simply because closeness creates repeated opportunities to interact. Repeated exposure then breeds familiarity, and via the mere-exposure effect familiarity tends to breed liking. Proximity is a precondition many biological and cognitive factors depend on — you cannot be attracted to, or discover similarity with, someone you never encounter.

items: **Key study (proximity): Festinger, Schachter & Back (1950).** AIM: examine how physical arrangement shapes friendship. PROCEDURE: mapped the friendships that formed among residents of a student housing complex (Westgate). FINDINGS: next-door neighbours became friends far more often than residents further apart, and 'functional distance' (e.g. living near stairwells or mailboxes, so you pass more people) predicted who became most popular.

**LINK (use it):** this supports the claim that a mundane environmental variable — where your front door happens to be — powerfully shapes who you form relationships with, independent of personality or attraction.

**Evaluation:** the study is a naturalistic field study (high ecological validity) but correlational and from a specific, homogeneous 1950s student population; the digital era, where relationships form online without physical proximity, is a strong contemporary limitation to raise.

sections.question: Planning task: You are asked, 'Discuss factors influencing the formation of personal relationships. [22]'. In under a minute, choose TWO factors and one study each, and write the single 'use' sentence for one of them.
sections.solution:
**A strong, examinable plan (depth beats coverage):**

1. **Factor 1 — biological (pheromones/MHC):** study = Wedekind (1995).
2. **Factor 2 — sociocultural (proximity/familiarity):** study = Festinger, Schachter & Back (1950).

These two contrast cleanly — an internal, physiological cause versus an external, environmental one — which sets up developed critical thinking (Criterion D) by comparison.

**The 'use' sentence for Festinger (turning description into evidence):**
'Because residents who lived nearest became friends far more often than distant ones, Festinger et al. show that proximity, a sociocultural variable, shapes relationship formation even before attraction or similarity can operate — supporting the claim that formation is enabled by opportunity, not just preference.'

Notice the sentence names the factor, states what the finding shows, and links it to the question. That link is the whole difference between Criterion C description and Criterion C USE.

sections.content: The role of communication in maintaining relationships

Once formed, relationships are sustained less by attraction than by how partners communicate. Two processes matter most. First, self-disclosure: gradually revealing personal information builds intimacy, and because disclosure is normally reciprocal, it signals trust — this is the engine of deepening closeness. Second, the QUALITY of everyday and conflict communication. John Gottman's longitudinal observational work on couples identified a cluster of corrosive patterns he called the Four Horsemen — Criticism (attacking character rather than behaviour), Contempt (mockery and disrespect), Defensiveness (refusing responsibility) and Stonewalling (emotional withdrawal). Of these, contempt is the strongest single predictor of eventual breakdown. Gottman also found that stable couples maintain a high ratio of positive to negative interactions even during conflict.

items: **Self-disclosure** builds intimacy when it is gradual and reciprocal; a mismatch (one partner over-discloses, the other withholds) undermines trust.
**Gottman's Four Horsemen** — Criticism, Contempt, Defensiveness, Stonewalling — are communication patterns that predict dissolution; contempt is the most damaging.
**LINK (use it):** because Gottman could predict later relationship outcomes from observing communication style, communication is shown to be causally central to maintenance, not merely a symptom.

**Evaluation:** Gottman's core data are correlational/longitudinal, so a third variable (e.g. chronic stress) could drive both the negative communication and the breakup; predictive accuracy from short samples has also been debated. Still, it usefully identifies specific, observable behaviours rather than vague 'poor communication'.

sections.content: Why relationships change or end: equity theory and breakdown models

Two complementary explanations dominate. Equity theory (Walster/Hatfield and colleagues, 1978) proposes that satisfaction depends on perceived FAIRNESS — the ratio of what each partner puts in to what they get out. Crucially, both being under-benefitted (giving more than you receive, which breeds resentment) and being over-benefitted (receiving more than you give, which breeds guilt) reduce satisfaction. When inequity is felt and cannot be restored, partners are motivated to change or leave the relationship. Duck's phase model of dissolution (1982) then describes HOW ending unfolds — as a process, not a single event — through four phases: the Intrapsychic phase (private dissatisfaction and brooding), the Dyadic phase (confronting the partner), the Social phase (telling friends and family, making the split public), and the Grave-dressing phase (constructing an acceptable story of why it ended).

items: **Equity theory (Walster et al., 1978):** perceived fairness, not just profit, predicts satisfaction; chronic inequity motivates change or breakdown.
**Duck's phase model (1982):** Intrapsychic → Dyadic → Social → Grave-dressing — dissolution as a staged process.
**LINK the two (use them together):** felt inequity can be the trigger that launches the Intrapsychic phase, showing the theories are complementary rather than competing — a strong Criterion D move.

**Evaluation:** equity theory may be more predictive in individualistic cultures, where relationships are seen as negotiated exchanges, than in collectivist contexts; Duck's model is descriptive, telling us the stages but not WHY breakdown begins — which is exactly the gap equity theory fills.

sections.question: Practice ERQ (8-mark style): 'Explain one theory of why relationships change or end.' Sketch a top-scoring paragraph structure using equity theory.
sections.solution:
**Point:** One explanation for why relationships end is a loss of equity — the perceived fairness between partners.

**Explain:** Equity theory (Walster/Hatfield et al., 1978) argues that satisfaction depends on the ratio of each partner's inputs to outcomes. Both under-benefitted partners (who feel resentment) and over-benefitted partners (who feel guilt) are less satisfied than partners in a balanced relationship, so felt inequity creates pressure to restore fairness or exit.

**Evidence + USE:** Research on couples finds that those who perceive their relationship as equitable report higher satisfaction and stability than those who feel over- or under-benefitted. This USES the evidence to support the theory's central prediction: it is fairness, not raw benefit, that keeps relationships intact — so when one partner chronically over-invests, dissatisfaction and breakdown follow.

**Link + critical note:** Equity theory therefore explains not just THAT relationships end but the mechanism — perceived injustice. A limitation to acknowledge is cultural: the exchange logic may fit individualistic, choice-based relationships better than collectivist or arranged ones, so the theory is powerful but bounded.

items: **Mislabelling the level of analysis** — calling proximity a 'biological' factor or pheromones a 'cognitive' one. Keep it clean: biological = attraction/pheromones/reward; cognitive = similarity/perception; sociocultural = proximity/familiarity/norms. This costs Criterion B (Knowledge).

**Arguing 'opposites attract'** — writing complementarity as your main claim. The supported factor is SIMILARITY; complementarity has weak evidence and belongs (if at all) as a critical counterpoint, not a thesis.

**Describing studies instead of USING them** — a perfect account of Wedekind or Festinger with no link sentence scores in Criterion B but leaves Criterion C stranded. Always add 'This supports/challenges the claim that...'.

**Evaluation by list** — tacking 'it's correlational, it's culturally biased, small sample' onto the end without linking each point to the specific study or argument. Criterion D rewards DEVELOPED critical thinking (explained and relevant), not a bolted-on checklist.

**Ignoring the command term** — writing a purely descriptive answer to a 'Discuss' or 'Evaluate' question. Without developed critical thinking the essay is capped in the lower bands however much knowledge it shows.

**Confusing equity with social exchange** — treating 'more rewards than costs' (exchange) as the same as 'fairness of the ratio' (equity). The distinction is exactly where the good critical points live.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB Paper 2 markband

sections.question: Discuss factors that influence the formation of personal relationships. [22]
sections.solution:
**Model essay**

**Thesis / focus.** The formation of personal relationships is best understood as the product of several interacting factors rather than any single cause. This essay argues that formation is enabled at two clearly distinct levels — a biological factor (pheromone-mediated attraction linked to the major histocompatibility complex, MHC) and a sociocultural factor (proximity and the familiarity it produces) — and that the most convincing account treats these as complementary: proximity supplies the opportunity, while biological mechanisms bias who, among those we encounter, we are drawn to. Two studies, Wedekind (1995) and Festinger, Schachter and Back (1950), are used to support and critically test this claim.

**Factor 1 — biological (pheromones/MHC).** At the biological level, attraction is partly shaped by physiology rather than conscious choice. The MHC is a cluster of immune-system genes thought to influence body odour, and the adaptive argument is that pairing with an MHC-dissimilar partner would give offspring a broader immune defence, so a scent-based preference for genetic difference could have been selected for. Wedekind (1995) tested this directly. The AIM was to establish whether MHC affects odour preference. In the PROCEDURE, 49 women rated the pleasantness of the odour of T-shirts that had each been worn for two nights by men who had been MHC-typed. The FINDINGS were that women preferred the odours of MHC-DISSIMILAR men — but strikingly, this preference reversed among women taking the contraceptive pill. USED as evidence, this supports the essay's claim that a biological, odour-mediated mechanism biases attraction toward genetically complementary partners, showing that formation is not purely a matter of deliberate preference. The pill reversal is doubly useful: it simultaneously supports a hormonal mechanism and warns that the mechanism is not fixed, which sets up evaluation.

**Factor 2 — sociocultural (proximity/familiarity).** A biological bias, however, can only operate on people we actually meet, which is where the sociocultural factor of proximity becomes decisive. Proximity creates repeated opportunities for interaction; repeated exposure breeds familiarity; and via the mere-exposure effect familiarity tends to breed liking. Festinger, Schachter and Back (1950) demonstrated this in a naturalistic field study. The AIM was to examine how physical arrangement shapes friendship formation. In the PROCEDURE, the researchers mapped the friendships that developed among residents of a student housing complex. The FINDINGS were that next-door neighbours became friends far more often than residents living further apart, and that 'functional distance' — for example living near stairwells or mailboxes, so that one passes more people — predicted who became most popular. USED as evidence, this supports the claim that formation is enabled by opportunity: a mundane environmental variable, where one's front door happens to be, shapes who we form relationships with even before attraction or similarity can operate.

**Developed critical thinking.** The two factors are strongest when read together rather than in competition. Festinger explains the OPPORTUNITY for relationships; Wedekind explains a bias in SELECTION among those opportunities — proximity and biology therefore operate at different stages, which is precisely why a single-factor explanation is inadequate. Methodologically, the studies differ in instructive ways. Wedekind's is a controlled experiment allowing a causal inference about odour, but it is artificial (rating strangers' T-shirts is not choosing a partner) and its sample is small and exclusively female, limiting generalisation. Festinger's field study has high ecological validity but is correlational and drawn from a homogeneous 1950s student population, and it faces a sharp contemporary challenge: in the digital era many relationships form online with no physical proximity at all, which bounds the factor's reach. A further critical point cuts across both: biological accounts risk reductionism and determinism, reducing a complex social act to genes or scent, while proximity risks environmental determinism. The resolution the essay defends — treating formation as multiply determined, with sociocultural opportunity and biological bias each necessary but neither sufficient — is more defensible than either factor alone.

**Conclusion.** Returning to the thesis: the evidence supports viewing relationship formation as layered. Festinger, Schachter and Back show that proximity supplies the conditions under which relationships can begin, while Wedekind shows that, among available others, biological mechanisms tilt attraction in particular directions. Each study also carries limitations — artificiality and a narrow sample in one, correlational design and dated context in the other — so the safest conclusion is not that one factor 'wins' but that formation is best explained by the interaction of sociocultural opportunity and biological predisposition, an account that also leaves room for the cognitive factor of similarity operating once interaction begins.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22)**

The ERQ is marked on five criteria: A Focus on the question (2), B Knowledge and understanding (6), C Use of research to support the answer (6), D Critical thinking (6), E Clarity and organisation (2).

- **A — Focus (2/2).** The introduction sets an explicit thesis (formation is multiply determined; two named factors) and the conclusion returns to it. The command term 'Discuss' is honoured by weighing the factors, not just listing them. Top band: the question is understood and the answer stays anchored to it throughout.
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6).** Both factors are explained accurately and in depth, terminology is precise (MHC, mere-exposure effect, functional distance), and the two levels of analysis are correctly labelled. Top band: knowledge is relevant, detailed and accurate.
- **C — Use of research (6/6).** Two studies are given with aim, procedure and findings AND each is followed by an explicit 'this supports the claim that...' link. This is the pivotal criterion: research is USED as evidence for the argument, not described and abandoned. Top band requires research that is explicitly and effectively used to support the response — met here.
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6).** The critical thinking is DEVELOPED, not listed: the two factors are compared (opportunity vs selection), methodological strengths and limitations are tied to each specific study, a contemporary challenge (online relationships) is raised, and the reductionism/determinism issue is resolved into a defended position. Top band: critical thinking is developed, balanced and organised.
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2).** The essay is structured (thesis → factor 1 → factor 2 → integrated critique → conclusion), each paragraph has a clear function, and ideas are expressed coherently. Top band: well organised and clearly expressed.

**Why this reaches the top bands rather than the middle:** a middle-band answer typically knows the same studies but only DESCRIBES them (losing C) and evaluates by bolted-on checklist (losing D). This answer moves up by doing two specific things — adding a 'use' sentence after every study, and DEVELOPING each critical point by tying it to a named study and to the thesis. Those two habits are the entire distance between 12–14/22 and 20–22/22.

sections.content: Press the Practice button below and write your own answer to this exact 22-mark question — our marking engine grades it against the same five-criterion markband and tells you which band you hit on A, B, C, D and E, and why. Watch for the two CAPS the engine enforces, which mirror the real exam: (1) the USE cap — if your studies are described but never linked to the argument, Criterion C is held in the lower bands no matter how accurate the description; and (2) the DEVELOPED cap — if a 'Discuss/Evaluate' answer is descriptive or evaluates by unlinked list, Criterion D is capped in the middle bands, which drags the whole essay down. Beating both caps is simple to say and hard to do: one 'this supports the claim that...' sentence per study, and one developed (explained + linked) critical point per paragraph.

Formation, communication and dissolution are one continuous story: the factors that draw people together (biological, cognitive, sociocultural) set the stage, communication determines whether the relationship is maintained, and equity and breakdown models explain how it unravels. Master the discipline this lesson trains — USING research and DEVELOPING critical thinking — and it transfers directly to every other Human Relationships topic (group dynamics, social responsibility) and to Paper 2 as a whole, where the same five criteria decide every essay.
